---
title: "Java - TP3"
description: "Multimedia player"
format:
  html:
    toc-expand: 3
number-sections: true
number-depth: 3
mermaid:
  theme: neutral
---

:::{.notes}
Ideas of Questions:

- About Stream
- pitest
- Interface?
:::

## Instructions

This lab will be covered in practical sessions 3 and 4.


### Launch service VSCode Java

- [ ] Restart the service from the last lab or Launch a new service [VScode-Java](https://datalab.sspcloud.fr/launcher/ide/vscode-python?name=vscode-java&version=2.5.2&s3=region-79669f20&init.personalInit=«https%3A%2F%2Fraw.githubusercontent.com%2Fludo2ne%2FENSAI-2A-Java%2Frefs%2Fheads%2Fmain%2Fdoc%2Ftp%2Fvscode-java.sh»&networking.user.enabled=true&autoLaunch=true){target="_blank"}

### Update your repository

Perhaps the repository used as a template has been updated since the last practical session.

- [ ] Follow the commands given in Lab 2 to update your repository

### Open Folder

In VSCode :

- [ ] File > Open Folder > tp3
  - or using: `code-server ~/work/ENSAI-2A-Java-TP/tp3/`

**tp3** must be the root folder in your explorer.


### When you've finished

- [ ] Save your work using git
- [ ] Pause or Delete your service


## Subject

This exercise involves designing and implementing a media management system in Java. You'll create classes for media types (songs, podcasts), playlists, and a media player. Focus on object-oriented principles, defining attributes, methods, and relationships. The goal is a functional system for managing and playing media.

Given files: `Song.java` and `pom.xml`

- [ ] Create the arborscence of a Maven project

::: {.callout-tip}
`mkdir -p folder/subfolder/subsubfolder` to create multiple nested directories with one command.
:::

## Questions

### Artist

In the Song class, we want to replace the String types for attributes singer, author, and composer with instances of an Artist class.

:::{style="background-color: #d7e5ecff;"}
```{mermaid}
classDiagram

class Artist {
     -firstName: String
     -lastName: String
     -nationality: String
     +toString(): String
}
```
:::

In the *toString()* method, concat "Artist" with firstName and lastName.

- [ ] Create `Artist` class
- [ ] Refactor Song class updating attributes types
  - We'll assume that the songs have a single singer, a single author, a single composer
- [ ] In Song class, create method *play()*, 
  - this method will print each word of lyrics
  - use the code below to add a brief delay between each word

```{.java}
try {
    Thread.sleep(100);
} catch (InterruptedException e) {
    Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
    System.err.println("Thread was interrupted");
}
```

- [ ] Create a class *Main* with a *main* method
- [ ] In the *main()* method, create an instance of Song and play it
  - use a real Song, singer and lyrics
  - you are allowed to leave author and composer blank
  - for the lyrics, you can limit them to a few hundred words

### Podcast

In your media player you will also read Podcasts :

:::{style="background-color: #d7e5ecff;"}
```{mermaid}
classDiagram

class Podcast {
    -title: String
    -host: String
    -topic: String
    -duration: int
    -year: int
    -subtitles: String
    +play()
}
```
:::

- [ ] Implement class `Podcast`
  - Use OOP to avoid code duplication
- [ ] Create method *play()*, printing subtitles
  - Bonus: You will notice that this method is very similar to Song's. Isn't there a better way?
- [ ] Implement the *toString()* method in the same way as for songs


### Pop, Rock, Electro

We want to add a new attribute to the Song to store the musical genres it is associated with.

- [ ] Create a structure to store musical genres
- [ ] Add 10 values of your choice
- [ ] Modify the Song class to add a list of musical genres to each song
- [ ] Don't forget to update the *main()* method


### Playlist

We now want to be able to create playlists with these songs and podcasts.

:::{style="background-color: #d7e5ecff;"}
```{mermaid}
classDiagram

class Playlist {
    -name: String
    -mediaList: List~Media~
    -totalDuration: int
    +addMedia(Media media)
    +removeMedia(Media media): bool
    +removeMedia(int index)
    +play(boolean random)
}
```
:::

- [ ] Create a `Playlist` class with a *name*, a *list of media* and *total duration*
- [ ] Develop the *addMedia()* method to add a media at the end of the playlist
- [ ] Create the *removeMedia(Media media)* method
  - it deletes occurences of a media if it exists in the playlist
- [ ] Create unit tests with Junit for these last two methods
- [ ] Instanciate the *removeMedia(Int i)* method to delete a media by index
- [ ] Develop a method for playing the playlist
  - Include a parameter to play the tracks randomly
- [ ] Add the option of copying the playlist


### Create some objects

In the main method:

- [ ] Create a new song (in addition to the one created at the beginning), as well as a Podcast
- [ ] Instantiate a playlist named *p1* and add these three media to it
- [ ] Copy your playlist and name it *p2*
- [ ] Remove a song from *p1*
- [ ] Print the total duration of *p1* and *p2*
- [ ] Play *p2*


### Feed a playlist

::: {.callout-tip title="Goal"}
The next step is to be able to add a set of media files to a playlist.

A JSON file is provided; the aim is to add all medias from this file into a playlist.
:::

- [ ] Is it a good idea to create a *loadJsonFile()* method in the Playlist class?
  - :bulb: Check SOLID principles

You'll need a tool to read JSON:

- [ ] Add dependency *org.json* in file *pom.xml*

Spoiler alert to the previous question, the answer was no:

- [ ] Create a class `MediaJsonLoader` with a method called *load()* returning a list of Medias

```{.java filename="MediaJsonLoader.java"}
package fr.ensai.mediaplayer;

import java.io.IOException;
import java.io.InputStream;
import java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets;
import java.util.List;

import org.json.JSONArray;
import org.json.JSONObject;

public class MediaJsonLoader {

    public static List<Media> load(String filename) {

        try (InputStream is = MediaJsonLoader.class
                .getClassLoader()
                .getResourceAsStream(filename)) {

            if (is == null) {
                throw new RuntimeException("File not found in resources: " + filename);
            }

            String content = new String(is.readAllBytes(), StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
            JSONArray array = new JSONArray(content);

            // Code Here

            // examples of use
            int i = 0;
            JSONObject json = array.getJSONObject(i);
            String title = json.getString("title");
            int year = json.getInt("year");
            String lyrics = json.optString("lyrics");

        } catch (IOException e) {
            throw new RuntimeException("Error reading file: " + filename, e);
        }

        return null;
    }
}
```

Let's use it in the main method:

- [ ] Load file *medias.json*
- [ ] Use fields to create songs and podcasts
  - Bonus1: Manage singers: `JSONObject singerJson = json.getJSONObject("singer");`
  - Bonus2: Manage musical genres: `JSONArray genresJson = json.optJSONArray("genres");`
- [ ] Add all medias to a playlist
  - Perhaps it would be a good idea to have a suitable method
- [ ] Play it


Let's continue to keep separate responsibilities.

The creation of a Media object should not be handled within the class `MediaJsonLoader`.

You'll need a class that can create either a song or a podcast from a JSON file.

- [ ] Create class `MediaFactory` and method *create()*

```{.java}
public class MediaFactory {
    public static Media create(JSONObject json) {
        // TODO
    }
}
```

- [ ] Refactor class `MediaJsonLoader` to use the factory

## End of the TP{.unnumbered}

- [ ] Commit and push
- [ ] Pause or delete your service
